title: Analysis of Cars
text:
Let’s investigate the relationship between fuel efficiency (mpg) and engine displacement (disp) from the mtcars dataset. Those variables have a correlation of r cor(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$disp) |> format(digits=2).


Here is a plot:

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pydataset import data

mtcars = data("mtcars")

plt.scatter(mtcars["mpg"], mtcars["disp"])
plt.xlabel("mpg")
plt.ylabel("disp")
plt.show()

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem






Note



In this section, connect the A/B test to a formal statistical framework. Address each of the following ideas:

Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their probability of signing up, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). Each visitor can either sign up (1) or not (0).
The quantity we care about is \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\), the difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample means, \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). (Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data






Note




Write a brief paragraph for the reader explaining that in the real world we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\), but for the purpose of this exercise we will set them ourselves so that we can study the behavior of our estimator when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).
Using a language of your choice, simulate 1,000 draws from each of the two Bernoulli distributions (2,000 total). Store the results so you can use them in the sections that follow.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers






Note




Write an introductory sentence or two explaining the Law of Large Numbers to the reader: as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Connect this back to our estimator — the LLN is what gives us confidence that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) when our sample is large enough.
Then demonstrate this visually. Using the simulated data from the previous section, compute the element-wise differences between the CTA A draws and the CTA B draws to get a vector of 1,000 differences. Calculate the cumulative average of that vector (i.e., the running mean after 1 draw, after 2 draws, …, after 1,000 draws) and plot it against the number of observations.
The plot should show a noisy average when only a few observations are included that settles down and converges toward the true difference of 0.04 as the sample size grows.
After the plot, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should take away from it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors






Note




Now that we “trust” our estimator, the next natural question is: how precise is it? We would like to report not just a point estimate of \(\theta\) but also a confidence interval that communicates our uncertainty.
Write a short introduction explaining the idea of bootstrapping to the reader. The bootstrap is a resampling method that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without relying on a closed-form formula. We resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.
Then carry out the following steps:

From your 1,000 CTA A draws and 1,000 CTA B draws, draw a bootstrap resample of size 1,000 (with replacement) from each group. Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat step 1 a total of 1,000 times to produce a vector of 1,000 bootstrapped estimates of \(\theta\).
Compute the standard deviation of those 1,000 values. This is your bootstrapped standard error.
Compare it to the analytical standard error. For the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples, the standard error formula is:


\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

Report both the bootstrapped standard error and the analytical standard error and comment on how close they are to each other. Then use the bootstrapped standard error to construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) and interpret it for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Note




Write a short introduction explaining the Central Limit Theorem to the reader: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of the difference in sample means) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).
After the plots, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should observe: at small sample sizes the histogram may look rough or asymmetric, but as \(n\) increases the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) becomes smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing






Note




Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.
Write a paragraph explaining what a hypothesis test is to the reader. We set up a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (i.e., the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate) and an alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\). The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
Then explain why we standardize \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic. We compute:

\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]

Walk the reader through the logic of this standardization step by step:

By the CLT, we know that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), the mean of that distribution is 0.
When we divide a Normal random variable by its standard deviation, we get a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
This means we know the distribution of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.

Explain to the reader why this is sometimes called a “t-test” even though the test statistic follows an approximate Normal distribution, not a t-distribution. The classical t-test arises when the data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. In our setting the data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand why.
Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website with a newsletter, and you want as many visitors as possible to sign up. On your landing page, you have a single “call to action” (CTA) — a short line of text that prompts visitors to subscribe. You’re currently testing two versions:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

Which one actually works better? Your gut feeling isn’t enough — you need data. That’s where an A/B test comes in.
In an A/B test, each visitor to your site is randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. By randomly splitting visitors into two groups, we ensure that any difference in sign-up rates between the groups can be attributed to the CTA itself, not to some other difference between the visitors. After collecting enough data, we compare the sign-up rates across the two groups and use statistical tools to decide whether any observed difference is real or just due to random chance.
In this post, we’ll walk through the key ideas from classical frequentist statistics that underpin a proper A/B test — using simulation to make each concept concrete and intuitive.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Imagine you run a website with a newsletter, and you want as many visitors as possible to sign up. On your landing page, you have a single “call to action” (CTA) — a short line of text that prompts visitors to subscribe. You’re currently testing two versions:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

Which one actually works better? Your gut feeling isn’t enough — you need data. That’s where an A/B test comes in.
In an A/B test, each visitor to your site is randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs. By randomly splitting visitors into two groups, we ensure that any difference in sign-up rates between the groups can be attributed to the CTA itself, not to some other difference between the visitors. After collecting enough data, we compare the sign-up rates across the two groups and use statistical tools to decide whether any observed difference is real or just due to random chance.
In this post, we’ll walk through the key ideas from classical frequentist statistics that underpin a proper A/B test — using simulation to make each concept concrete and intuitive.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
To analyze our A/B test rigorously, we need to frame it as a formal statistical problem.
Each visitor to our site either signs up (outcome = 1) or doesn’t (outcome = 0). This binary outcome is naturally modeled as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution: a coin flip with some probability \(\pi\) of landing heads (signing up). Because the two CTAs might influence visitor behavior differently, we allow the sign-up probability to differ between groups:

Visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\)
Visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\)

The quantity we actually care about is the difference in sign-up rates:
\[\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\]
If \(\theta > 0\), CTA A is better. If \(\theta < 0\), CTA B is better. If \(\theta = 0\), neither outperforms the other.
Of course, we never observe \(\pi_A\) or \(\pi_B\) directly — we only see the outcomes for the visitors in our experiment. So we estimate \(\theta\) using the difference in sample proportions:
\[\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_A - \hat\pi_B\]
where \(\bar{X}_A\) is the fraction of CTA A visitors who signed up, and \(\bar{X}_B\) is the same for CTA B. This estimator \(\hat\theta\) is the central object of our analysis. The rest of this post explores its statistical properties through simulation.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In the real world, we would never know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\) — that’s precisely why we run the experiment. But for the purposes of this exercise, we get to play God: we’ll set the true parameters ourselves, run our statistical methods, and verify that they behave as theory predicts.
We’ll assume:
\[\pi_A = 0.22, \quad \pi_B = 0.18, \quad \theta = \pi_A - \pi_B = 0.04\]
So CTA A is genuinely better by 4 percentage points. We simulate 1,000 visitors per group — 2,000 observations total.


Code
np.random.seed(42)

pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18
n    = 1000

draws_A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
draws_B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

print(f"Sample proportion for CTA A : {draws_A.mean():.4f}  (true: {pi_A})")
print(f"Sample proportion for CTA B : {draws_B.mean():.4f}  (true: {pi_B})")
print(f"Estimated difference θ̂     : {draws_A.mean() - draws_B.mean():.4f}  (true: 0.04)")


Sample proportion for CTA A : 0.2160  (true: 0.22)
Sample proportion for CTA B : 0.1780  (true: 0.18)
Estimated difference θ̂     : 0.0380  (true: 0.04)


Our sample proportions are very close to the true values of 0.22 and 0.18 — a reassuring sign. But why can we trust them? That’s what the next section is about.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) tells us that as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the true population mean. In our setting, this means \(\hat\pi_A \to \pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B \to \pi_B\) as we collect more data — and therefore \(\hat\theta \to \theta\).
The LLN is the foundational reason we trust our estimator: given enough data, it will get arbitrarily close to the truth. But how much data is “enough”? The plot below makes this tangible.
We compute the element-wise difference between our CTA A and CTA B draws, then track the running (cumulative) average of those differences as we add one observation at a time.


Code
diffs       = draws_A - draws_B
running_avg = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n + 1)











With only a handful of observations, the running average swings wildly. But as the sample size grows, the noise dies down and the estimate settles close to the true value of 0.04 (the red dashed line). This is the LLN at work: more data means a more reliable estimate.
The practical takeaway for A/B testing is clear: small samples can be deeply misleading. If you had stopped at 50 visitors per group, you might have drawn completely wrong conclusions. Size matters.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Knowing that our estimator \(\hat\theta\) is close to the truth on average is useful, but it raises a follow-up question: how close? A point estimate without a measure of uncertainty is incomplete. We need a confidence interval.
One elegant way to quantify the uncertainty in \(\hat\theta\) is the bootstrap. The idea: instead of deriving a formula for the variability of our estimator, we use the data itself to simulate what would happen if we ran the experiment many times.
Here’s the procedure:

From our 1,000 CTA A observations and 1,000 CTA B observations, draw a resample of size 1,000 with replacement from each group.
Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) on the resample.
Repeat 1,000 times. The standard deviation of the 1,000 bootstrapped \(\hat\theta^*\) values is our bootstrapped standard error.



Code
B = 1000
boot_estimates = np.empty(B)

for b in range(B):
    resample_A        = np.random.choice(draws_A, size=n, replace=True)
    resample_B        = np.random.choice(draws_B, size=n, replace=True)
    boot_estimates[b] = resample_A.mean() - resample_B.mean()

se_boot = boot_estimates.std()

# Analytical SE for difference in two independent Bernoulli proportions
pi_hat_A = draws_A.mean()
pi_hat_B = draws_B.mean()
se_analytical = np.sqrt(pi_hat_A * (1 - pi_hat_A) / n +
                        pi_hat_B * (1 - pi_hat_B) / n)

theta_hat = pi_hat_A - pi_hat_B
ci_lower  = theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot
ci_upper  = theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot

print(f"Point estimate (θ̂)          : {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"Bootstrapped standard error  : {se_boot:.4f}")
print(f"Analytical standard error    : {se_analytical:.4f}")
print(f"95% Confidence interval      : [{ci_lower:.4f}, {ci_upper:.4f}]")


Point estimate (θ̂)          : 0.0380
Bootstrapped standard error  : 0.0177
Analytical standard error    : 0.0178
95% Confidence interval      : [0.0034, 0.0726]


The bootstrapped and analytical standard errors are very close to each other — the bootstrap “discovers” the right answer without needing a formula, purely from the data.
Our 95% confidence interval says: given our data, we estimate that CTA A’s sign-up rate exceeds CTA B’s by somewhere between {ci_lower100:.1f}% and {ci_upper100:.1f}% percentage points. Since the entire interval sits above zero, this is strong evidence that CTA A genuinely outperforms CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The bootstrap gave us a practical tool for computing standard errors, but there’s a deeper theoretical result underpinning our ability to do inference: the Central Limit Theorem (CLT).
The CLT states that regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows. In our case, even though individual outcomes are Bernoulli (binary 0/1), the distribution of \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will look like a bell curve for large enough \(n\).
To see this in action, we simulate 1,000 experiments at four different sample sizes — \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\) — and plot the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) each time.


Code
sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
n_sim        = 1000

clt_results = {}
for ns in sample_sizes:
    theta_hats = np.array([
        np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, ns).mean() - np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, ns).mean()
        for _ in range(n_sim)
    ])
    clt_results[ns] = theta_hats











The progression is striking. At \(n = 25\), the histogram is jagged and irregular. By \(n = 100\), a clear bell shape is emerging. At \(n = 500\), the distribution is smooth and symmetric around the true value of 0.04. This is the CLT in action — and it’s why serious A/B tests require hundreds of observations per group, not dozens.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Now that we’ve seen the CLT in action, we can use it to run a formal hypothesis test.
We set up two competing hypotheses:

Null hypothesis \(H_0\): \(\theta = 0\) (the two CTAs produce identical sign-up rates)
Alternative hypothesis \(H_1\): \(\theta \neq 0\) (the CTAs differ)

The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\). We do this by standardizing \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic:
\[z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}\]
The logic: by the CLT, \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), its mean is 0. Dividing by the standard deviation gives a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\) — whose distribution we know exactly, allowing us to compute a p-value.
A note on terminology: this is sometimes called a t-test, but strictly speaking that label applies when data are drawn from a Normal distribution. Our data are Bernoulli, so the CLT gives us approximate Normality — making this more accurately a z-test. For large samples the distinction is negligible.


Code
z_stat  = theta_hat / se_analytical
p_value = 2 * stats.norm.sf(abs(z_stat))

print(f"Estimated difference (θ̂) : {theta_hat:.4f}")
print(f"Standard error            : {se_analytical:.4f}")
print(f"z-statistic               : {z_stat:.3f}")
print(f"p-value                   : {p_value:.4f}")


Estimated difference (θ̂) : 0.0380
Standard error            : 0.0178
z-statistic               : 2.139
p-value                   : 0.0325


With a p-value well below 0.05, we have strong evidence against \(H_0\). If the two CTAs were truly identical, there would be only a tiny chance of observing a difference as large as ours by chance alone. We reject \(H_0\) and conclude that CTA A significantly outperforms CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
Here’s a surprising and useful fact: the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression.
We stack our data into a single dataset with:

\(Y_i\): the outcome (1 = signed up, 0 = didn’t)
\(D_i\): an indicator for CTA (1 = CTA A, 0 = CTA B)

And fit:
\[Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i\]
The interpretation is clean:

\(\beta_0 = E[Y_i \mid D_i = 0] = \pi_B\) — the CTA B sign-up rate
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1 = E[Y_i \mid D_i = 1] = \pi_A\) — the CTA A sign-up rate
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta\)



Code
Y = np.concatenate([draws_A, draws_B])
D = np.concatenate([np.ones(n), np.zeros(n)])
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(2 * n), D])

beta      = np.linalg.lstsq(X, Y, rcond=None)[0]
beta0, beta1 = beta

residuals = Y - X @ beta
sigma2    = residuals @ residuals / (2 * n - 2)
cov_beta  = sigma2 * np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X)
se_beta1  = np.sqrt(cov_beta[1, 1])
t_stat_reg = beta1 / se_beta1
p_val_reg  = 2 * stats.t.sf(abs(t_stat_reg), df=2 * n - 2)

print(f"β̂₀ (CTA B mean)  : {beta0:.4f}   (true π_B = 0.18)")
print(f"β̂₁ (difference)  : {beta1:.4f}   (matches θ̂ = {theta_hat:.4f})")
print(f"SE(β̂₁)           : {se_beta1:.4f}")
print(f"t-statistic       : {t_stat_reg:.3f}")
print(f"p-value           : {p_val_reg:.4f}")


β̂₀ (CTA B mean)  : 0.1780   (true π_B = 0.18)
β̂₁ (difference)  : 0.0380   (matches θ̂ = 0.0380)
SE(β̂₁)           : 0.0178
t-statistic       : 2.138
p-value           : 0.0327


The regression coefficient \(\hat\beta_1\) matches our earlier \(\hat\theta\) exactly, and the p-values agree closely. A minor difference in the standard error arises because OLS assumes a pooled variance across groups — for large samples this distinction is negligible.
Why does this equivalence matter? Because the regression framework generalizes naturally. Add covariates to control for user characteristics, add indicator variables to test more than two CTAs, or add interaction terms to check whether effects differ across devices. The t-test is a special case of regression, and understanding this connection unlocks a much more powerful toolkit.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Your boss is impatient. She doesn’t want to wait until all 1,000 visitors per group have been observed. Instead, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors — peeking at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\) — and stop early if any peek is significant.
This sounds reasonable. It’s not.
Each test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) carries a 5% false positive rate. When you run 10 such tests on accumulating data, you get 10 chances to make a mistake. The overall probability of at least one false rejection climbs well above 5%.
To see exactly how bad the situation is, we simulate 10,000 experiments in a world where the null is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\), so any “significant” result is a false positive.


Code
np.random.seed(123)

n_experiments   = 10_000
peeks           = np.arange(100, 1001, 100)
false_positives = 0

for _ in range(n_experiments):
    A = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, 1000)
    B = np.random.binomial(1, 0.20, 1000)

    for peek in peeks:
        a_sub = A[:peek]
        b_sub = B[:peek]
        p_a   = a_sub.mean()
        p_b   = b_sub.mean()
        se_p  = np.sqrt(p_a * (1 - p_a) / peek + p_b * (1 - p_b) / peek)
        if se_p == 0:
            continue
        z    = (p_a - p_b) / se_p
        pval = 2 * stats.norm.sf(abs(z))
        if pval < 0.05:
            false_positives += 1
            break

empirical_fpr = false_positives / n_experiments
print(f"Nominal false positive rate (single test) : 5.0%")
print(f"Empirical false positive rate (peeking)   : {empirical_fpr*100:.1f}%")


Nominal false positive rate (single test) : 5.0%
Empirical false positive rate (peeking)   : 19.9%


The empirical false positive rate balloons to around **{empirical_fpr*100:.1f}%** — far above the nominal 5%. In other words, even when there is truly no difference between the CTAs, we’d incorrectly declare a winner in roughly one out of every four or five experiments.
This is why pre-registration matters in A/B testing: decide your sample size before collecting data, run the test once at the end, and commit to that result. If you want the flexibility to stop early, you need more sophisticated methods — such as sequential testing or Bayesian approaches — that properly account for the multiple looks. Classical frequentist tests applied naively with repeated peeking will mislead you far more often than you think.
